concerns.text: The plan proposes 5 repetitions per configuration for Kruskal-Wallis H-test. With only 5 samples per group (3 groups), the statistical power to detect anything but massive effect sizes is extremely low. The plan acknowledges this limitation but offers no mitigation beyond 'reporting effect sizes,' which does not solve the inability to reject the null hypothesis for realistic performance differences. This risks a Type II error (false negative) where real differences are missed.

The plan targets 225 benchmark configurations (3 impls × 5 sizes × 3 FPRs × 5 reps). Running these sequentially or even with limited parallelism on a 2-CPU runner within a 6-hour window is highly risky. If individual runs (especially 1M elements) take >10 minutes, the total time exceeds the limit. The plan lacks a strategy for dynamic load balancing or early termination of non-informative runs to ensure the full experimental matrix is completed.

The decision to use synthetic data (random strings) instead of the specified 'Enron Email Corpus' and 'Google 10000 English' introduces a construct validity risk. While Bloom filter performance is theoretically content-agnostic, hash function behavior can vary with string length distribution and character entropy. Random strings (5-20 chars) may not stress the hash functions or memory allocators in the same way as real-world text (e.g., long email subjects, variable lengths), potentially skewing latency and memory overhead results.
